mcqs.question: In the Nearest Centroid Classifier, what is stored for each class after training?

options.text: All training instances of that class
The centroid (mean vector) of training instances
The median vector of training instances

mcqs.explanation: NCC computes and stores the centroid (mean vector) for each class by averaging all training samples belonging to that class. This makes it memory-efficient compared to instance-based methods.

mcqs.question: For a two-class NCC problem with class centroids c₁ = [2,3] and c₂ = [8,7], to which class will a point x = [5,5] be assigned using Euclidean distance?

options.text: Class 1 because the centroid is closer to [5,5]

mcqs.explanation: Using Euclidean distance: d(x,c₁) = √((5-2)² + (5-3)²) = √(9+4) = √13 ≈ 3.61. d(x,c₂) = √((5-8)² + (5-7)²) = √(9+4) = √13 ≈ 3.61. They are equidistant, so typically we assign to either class or use tie-breaking rules. Wait - both distances are actually equal here: √13 for both. In practice, this is a tie.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT an assumption of the Nearest Centroid Classifier?

options.text: Classes have equal variance in all directions

mcqs.explanation: NCC assumes linear separability, spherical class distributions (equal variance in all directions), and continuous features. It does NOT assume features follow a normal distribution - that's a different classifier assumption (like Gaussian Naive Bayes).

mcqs.question: What happens to the NCC decision boundary when two class centroids move closer together?

options.text: The decision boundary moves closer to the midpoint
The decision boundary moves closer to the farther centroid

mcqs.explanation: The decision boundary is the perpendicular bisector of the line connecting the two centroids. As centroids move closer, the boundary remains at their midpoint but the margin of separation decreases, making classification less reliable.

mcqs.question: How does adding outliers affect the Nearest Centroid Classifier?

options.text: Outliers have no effect on NCC since it uses all data equally
Outliers can significantly shift the centroid, degrading performance
Outliers improve NCC performance by providing more information
Outliers only affect k-NN, not NCC

mcqs.explanation: The mean (centroid) is highly sensitive to outliers. A single extreme value can pull the centroid significantly toward it, causing misclassification of other points. This is a major limitation of NCC compared to more robust methods.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of shrinkage regularization in NCC?

options.text: To increase the effect of outliers on centroids
To pull class centroids toward the global mean and reduce overfitting
To make the decision boundary non-linear
To increase the number of stored centroids

mcqs.explanation: Shrinkage regularization pulls class centroids toward the global mean using the formula: cᵢ(shrunk) = (1-α)cᵢ + α(c_global). This reduces variance and overfitting, especially useful in high-dimensional spaces with limited samples.

mcqs.question: Consider a 3-class NCC with centroids at [0,0], [10,0], and [0,10]. Using Euclidean distance, classify point [5,1].
What is the space complexity of NCC compared to k-NN?
mcqs.explanation: NCC stores only k centroids of dimension d: O(k×d). k-NN stores all n training samples: O(n×d). For large datasets, NCC is much more memory-efficient.

mcqs.question: The Nearest Centroid Classifier produces which type of decision boundary?
mcqs.explanation: NCC produces a linear decision boundary - a hyperplane perpendicular to the line connecting class centroids and passing through the midpoint. This is because classification is based on linear distance to centroids.

mcqs.question: When will the Nearest Centroid Classifier fail even if classes are well-separated?

options.text: When classes have different variances (one is more spread out)
When training data is very large
When using Manhattan distance instead of Euclidean
When there are more features than samples

mcqs.explanation: NCC assumes spherical (equal covariance) class distributions. When classes have different variances, the nearest centroid rule fails because it doesn't account for class-specific spread. The decision boundary should favor the class with smaller variance.

mcqs.question: In the Rocchio classifier (NCC variant for text classification), what does each 'class' represent?

options.text: A prototype/ideal document for each category

mcqs.explanation: The Rocchio classifier computes a prototype (ideal) document vector for each class by averaging document vectors in that class. New documents are classified by finding the nearest prototype. It's commonly used in information retrieval and text classification.

mcqs.question: What is the prediction time complexity for NCC with k classes and d features?
mcqs.explanation: To predict, we compute distance from the test point to each of k centroids. Each distance computation takes O(d), so total is O(k × d). This is independent of training set size n.

mcqs.question: Which distance metric is most commonly used in NCC?
mcqs.explanation: Euclidean distance is the most common, but cosine similarity is also used, especially in text/high-dimensional NLP tasks. The choice affects the shape of decision boundaries.

mcqs.question: A company uses NCC to classify customer complaints into categories. They have 5 complaint categories and 50 features. What is stored after training?
mcqs.explanation: NCC stores only the class centroids: one 50-dimensional centroid per class. That's 5 × 50 = 250 values total, regardless of how many training samples exist. This is extremely memory-efficient.

mcqs.question: What happens to NCC performance when dimensionality increases (curse of dimensionality)?

options.text: Performance always improves with more features
Distance becomes less meaningful as points become equidistant
NCC is immune to curse of dimensionality
Centroids become more accurate with more features

mcqs.explanation: In high dimensions, all points tend to become approximately equidistant from each other (distance concentration), making the nearest centroid rule less discriminative. This is the curse of dimensionality affecting NCC.
